spec_match.rationale: Installs a real same-origin payment handler and resolves PaymentRequest.show() with success / AbortError / OperationError on demand — the precise spec contract.

interactive.rationale: Install + canMakePayment + three scenario buttons all drive real browser API calls; result panel reformats with the actual err.name and err.message.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb promises `installs a same-origin payment handler and requests success/AbortError/OperationError outcomes on demand` — delivered.

edge_cases.rationale: Only three outcomes are exposed at this level. The spec also distinguishes NotSupportedError (no matching method) and SecurityError (insecure origin / disallowed feature policy); neither is reachable here, even though the lower demos exercise sub-flavours of OperationError.

design_system.rationale: Variables-only colours and a correct button override pair, but the scenario cards use unicode glyphs `✓ ✕ ⚠` as titles — the convention is inline SVG.

openQuestions.title: Replace ✓ ✕ ⚠ titles with inline SVG icons
openQuestions.detail: Scenario card h3s lead with unicode glyphs. Project rule: inline SVG, never emoji or unicode glyphs, for UI elements. Replace with three small SVGs (checkmark, cross, alert triangle) sized to the h3 font.

openQuestions.title: Add a NotSupportedError scenario
openQuestions.detail: Triggered by passing a method URL the handler does not register, this rejects in `new PaymentRequest(...)` before show() even runs. It is a useful contrast to AbortError because the same `err.name` discriminator is still the right pattern — but the moment of failure is different.

openQuestions.title: Show pre-Chrome-149 collapse
openQuestions.detail: The feature exists because previously the OperationError case collapsed to AbortError. Add a `simulate pre-149` toggle that swallows the handler's OperationError and rethrows as AbortError, so the reader sees what the change actually changed.

openQuestions.title: Expose the raw `err` object alongside the friendly summary
openQuestions.detail: The result panel writes `err.name === 'AbortError' → true` etc, but never dumps the actual DOMException instance. A small `console-like` block under the result with the JSON-ish `{ name, message, code, stack? }` would let developers inspect what they get back.

summary: The cleanest of the six concepts for this feature — clear isolation of the three outcomes, real handler, correct discriminator pattern. Two small improvements: real SVG icons and a NotSupportedError tab.
